Dear Mr. Hallabrin:
I would like to urge your most expeditious handling of an opinion
request recently filed with you by the city Attorney of the city
of I'rilpitas concerning possible confrict-of-interest 
issues
arising from proposed generar plan and zoning amendments. The
City is stymied on taking any action until the relevant questions
arising under the Po1itical Reform Act are resolved.
I would like to emphasize
LD

action.


Gitguf Silpitur
455 E. Calaveras Blvd.
Milpitas, California 95035
April 2, 1992
Mr. Scott Hallabrin
Counsel, Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804
Re: Request for Advice. Government Code Section 831 14(b)
Hillside General PIan Amendment and Zoning Ordinance Revisions
Dear Mr. Hallabrin:
I am requesting formal wriften advice on behalf of five City ol Milpitas elected
and appointed otficials, pursuant to Government Code Sec. 831 14(b). The
elected officials and their mailing addresses are:
Vice Mayor James Lawson
2175 Fieldcrest Drive, Milpitas, CA 95035
Councilmember Barbara Lee
1934 Shenandoah Ave., Milpitas, CA 95035
The appointed oflicials are the following Planning Commissioners:
Mr. Urmas Meri
'1374 Columbus Circle, Milpitas, CA 95035
Ms. Annell Spencer
1062 Nicklaus Avenue, Milpitas, CA 95035
Ms. Patricia Dixon
1933 Grand Teton Drive, Milpitas, CA 95035
On March 30, 1992, Mr. Lawson and Ms. Lee asked me to solicit this advice on
their behalf. At the Planning Commission meeting of March 30, I was requested
to solicit this advice on behalf of Mr. Meri, Ms. Spencer and Ms. Dixon.
This request relates to a pending decision by the Planning Commission and
City Council on a General Plan Amendment, zone change, and zoning
ordinance revisions for the Hillside area of the City of Milpitas. I am enclosing a
C>
@
=t
a-D
,\.t
An Equsl Opporiunity Employer


Mr. Scott Hallabrin
Page 2
map prepared by the City of Milpitas Planning Division which shows the current
Hillside area and the location of the otficials' real property.
A potential conllici of interest arises concerning these otficials' ownership of
property near the Hillside area. As is evident from the enclosed map, none ot
these oflicials have residences in the current or proposed Hillside area.
Therefore, these residences are not subject to the decisions on the Hillside
area. Although these residences are within 2500'of the Hillside boundary, they
are unlikely to be atfected by the proposed General Plan Amondment or zone
changes since these homes are in established subdivisions and have no
'unique" characteristics which would be alfected by the proposed changes.
I would, theretore, appreciale your review as to the "public generally" exception
to the regulations because lhe etfecl o, zoning decisions on their financial
interests will be substaniially the same as the etfect on a signilicant segment of
the public (California Code ol Regulations, Title 2, Section 18703).
The Cily oI Milpitas has a population ol approximately 52,000 and is 13.6
square miles. The incorporated Hillside area, as delined by the City's current
zoning ordinance consists of four square miles, three ol which are privately
owned, and one ol which is owned by Santa Clara County. All of the
individuals named in this letter are in a position to influence and participate in
making decisions related to the proposed General Plan Amendment and
Hillside Ordinance revisions. The Planning Commissioners will make
recommendations to the City Council on the General Plan Amendment, zone
change and ordinance amendments. The Councilmembers will make the linal
decisions relating to adoption ol lhe General Plan Amendment, zone change,
and Hillside Ordinance.
The development ol the Hillsides has been a major land use issue for the City
as evidenced by the amount of time and study ol development regulalions to
dals. The major issues 16lated to Hillside Development include density,
minimum lot sizes, density transler credits, open space easements, lloor area
ratio requirements, and site and architectural controls.
The City Council on June 6, 1989, appointed an ad hoc sevenleen momber
Hillside Review Committss whose task was to review the Cily's Hillside
development regulations and make recommendations for proposed changes.
After meeting over forty times during a two and one-half year period, the
majority ol the Committee lorwarded a report to the City Council. A minority
r€port was also prepared and sent to the City Council on September 3, 1991.
The City Council directed the Planning Commission to hold public hearings and
make recommendations on ths Hillside R€view Commitlee's reporls. These
hearings occurrsd in Oclob€r and November 199'1. On January 21, 1992, the
City Council directed the Planning Commission to set a hearing on the


proposed General Plan Amendment and Hillside Ordinance text amendments.
At the January 21 , 1992 meeting, the City Council directed the City Attorney to
prepare an interim ordinance imposing a moratorium on the acceptance of all
applications for development in the Hillside area. Ordinance No. 38.668 was
adopted as an urgency measure on January 23,1992.. The moratorium was
subsequently extended to January 23, '1993 by Ordinance No. 38.669 adopted
on March 3, 1992. The proposed General Plan Amendment, zone change, and
ordinance revisions were scheduled for a headng before the Planning
Commission on March 30, 1992. At that time, the Commission decided to
request this advice and continue the matter pending your review.
The conllict of interest laws were created to prevent a public otficial from having
an unfair advantage or exercising undue influence by virtue of that otficial's
position. The regulations interpreting the conflict of interest laws take into
consideration factors such as the impact ol a decision on a signilicant segment
oI the public and allow public otficials to panicipate in the decision making
process even when the decision could have a reasonably foreseeable material
linancial ellsct on the otficials' intersst so long as that decision atfects the public
generally in substantially the same manner (2 C.C.R. S18703). The "Public
Generally' exception does not just apply to the total population ol a jurisdiction,
it can apply to a signiticant segment ol the public.
The Hillside area oI Milpitas is obviously very visible and of concern to all the
citizens of the City. The interest in preserving this unique, scenic assel has
been part oI the tradition and history o, the entire community. The problems ol
traffic congestion, commerce and developmenl are of general concern and the
Planning Commission and City Council have attempted to balance lhese
concerns with those ol orderly and regulated hillside development. Clearly, the
at ected property owners are not represenlative of any particular interest group.
Their commonality is based on ownership of homes within a 2500' radius of this
hillside districl.
The Cily would like to move loMard on these matters as soon as possible and
we contsmplate action as soon as this advice is received. I would greatly
Mr. Scott Hallabrin
Page 3
*The January 21, 1992 City Council meeting was adjourned to January 23,
1992 for consideration ol the proposed moratorium.


Mr. Scott Hallabrin
Page 4
approciate an expediled response so that lcan advise the officials on whose
behall this advice is sought. lf lcan provide additional inlormation which would
be oI assistance, please let me know.
Very truly yours,
/b.**/./--r*l*-
Sandra S. Faith{ull
City Attorney

sections.question: Whether five City of Milpitas elected and appointed officials (Vice Mayor James Lawson, Councilmember Barbara Lee, and Planning Commissioners Urmas Meri, Annell Spencer, and Patricia Dixon) have a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act in participating in decisions regarding a proposed Hillside area General Plan Amendment and zoning ordinance revisions, given their ownership of residential property within 2500 feet of the Hillside boundary.
sections.question_synthetic: The City of Milpitas seeks FPPC advice on whether five officials can participate in Hillside area zoning decisions despite owning nearby residential property, arguing the decisions affect the public generally rather than the officials' financial interests uniquely.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is an incoming request (from City of Milpitas) to FPPC, not an FPPC response. Includes cover letter from State Senator Dan McCorquodale requesting expedited handling. Two documents presented: Senator's letter of April 20, 1992 and City Attorney's formal advice request of April 2, 1992. No CONCLUSION section exists as this is a request, not a response.

embedding.summary: The City of Milpitas City Attorney requests formal written advice from the FPPC on potential conflict-of-interest issues for five elected and appointed officials regarding their participation in decisions on a proposed General Plan Amendment and zoning ordinance revisions for the Hillside area, invoking the 'public generally' exception under California Code of Regulations Section 18703.
